coredata.dc:title: Steering interfacial charge kinetics: Synergizing cocatalyst roles of Ti3C2 Mx (MXene) and NCDs for superior photocatalytic performance over TiO2

coredata.dc:description: Steering the kinetics of photoexcited electron-hole pairs to achieve the elongated lifetime of carrier is one of key strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance. Here, 0D/2D/2D NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx photocatalyst is fabricated for the first time via hydrothermal route, the mixed group terminated MXene (Ti3C2 Mx , M = -O, –OH, -F) and N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are exploited as an electron acceptor and hole mediator, respectively, enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanosheets by steering interfacial charge kinetics. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) degradation of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are ∼ 4 and 9 times higher than those of TiO2, respectively. UPS and DFT calculation results determine that the work function (Φ) of Ti3C2 Mx as 4.16 eV and 3.63 eV, respectively, which are larger than that of bare Ti3C2 (Φ = 3.55 eV), while the work function of TiO2 and NCDs are 3.98 and 3.09 eV, respectively. The results of charge transfer dynamics reveal that the photocatalytic enhancement of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx is dominantly attributed to the synergizing cocatalyst roles of Ti3C2 Mx (MXene) and NCDs. The insight into the surface termination dominated electronic property of MXene and synergistic steering the interfacial charge kinetics is an effective way to fabricate high performance MXene-based photocatalysts.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Steering the kinetics of photoexcited electron-hole pairs to achieve the elongated lifetime of carrier is one of key strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance. Here, 0D/2D/2D NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx photocatalyst is fabricated for the first time via hydrothermal route, the mixed group terminated MXene (Ti3C2 Mx , M = -O, –OH, -F) and N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are exploited as an electron acceptor and hole mediator, respectively, enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanosheets by steering interfacial charge kinetics. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) degradation of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are ∼ 4 and 9 times higher than those of TiO2, respectively. UPS and DFT calculation results determine that the work function (Φ) of Ti3C2 Mx as 4.16 eV and 3.63 eV, respectively, which are larger than that of bare Ti3C2 (Φ = 3.55 eV), while the work function of TiO2 and NCDs are 3.98 and 3.09 eV, respectively. The results of charge transfer dynamics reveal that the photocatalytic enhancement of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx is dominantly attributed to the synergizing cocatalyst roles of Ti3C2 Mx (MXene) and NCDs. The insight into the surface termination dominated electronic property of MXene and synergistic steering the interfacial charge kinetics is an effective way to fabricate high performance MXene-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy crisis and ecological environmental problems are challenging and critical issues to sustainable development [1,2]. Solar-driven photocatalysis has been considered as a green way in solving energy shortage and environmental pollution [3,4]. Among various kinds of photocatalytic materials, TiO2 has been regarded as one of the most promising photocatalytic materials in the future because of its suitable band edge positions for hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions, low price, environmental benignity, high reserves abundance, and stable chemical property [5,6]. However, the photogenerated charge separation efficiency and quantum efficiency of TiO2 are ultralow due to the extremely short lifetime (ps to ns) of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which severely restrict its practical applications [7,8].

At present, cocatalyst engineering has been regarded as the most effective strategy to improve the quantum efficiency [9,10]. Proper cocatalysts loaded on TiO2 could steer interfacial charge kinetics and promote the photocatalytic courses [11]. Novel carbon materials, such as carbon fiber [12], carbon nanotubes [13], graphene [14], and polypyrrole [15], have displayed promising potentials as the cocatalysts of TiO2 for enhanced carrier transport and improved light adsorption performance.

MXenes, a novel type and rapidly growing family of two-dimensional transition metal carbides (nitrides) [16], have been regarded as an excellent platform to fabricate photocatalysts due to its high mechanical strength, excellent metallic electrical conductivity, and hydrophilic nature [17]. Guided by photoelectrocatalysis, Yu’s group designed a series of novel MXene-based materials, which were applied for hydrogen evolution [18], oxygen evolution [19], and CO2 reduction [20–22] reactions. Tian et al. designed and prepared 1D/2D CdS/Ti3C2 [23] and 2D/2D CdS/Ti3C2 [24] composite photocatalyst for efficient hydrogen production from water splitting under visible light. In addition, Lv et al. [25], Niu et al. [26], Yang et al. [27] and Meng et al. [28] successfully fabricated ultrathin 2D/2D Ti3C2/g-C3N4 heterojunctions by direct calcination and self-assembly route, respectively. They found that the heterojunctions exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity attributed to an efficient spatial charge separation. Meanwhile, MXene-based photocatalysts can effectively separate the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby increasing the lifetime of charge carriers through dual Schottky junction [29], Z-scheme [30,31], S-scheme [22,32–34] and“M−scheme” [35] strategies.

Furthermore, precisely steering interfacial charge kinetics via multiple cocatalysts loadings on TiO2 can further improve its photocatalytic performance [58]. Recently, another carbon material, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with a diameter of 2–10 nm, have attracted widespread attention as a metal-free cocatalyst, which retain a typically π-π* stacking transitions aromatic sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon [59], and extend the visible photo-adsorption and accelerate the shuttle of photogenerated carriers by nitrogen dopants [60]. It is worth mentioning that NCDs (or CDs) mainly act as electron acceptors instead of hole acceptors in photocatalysis according to previous reports [61–63]. Considering the multiple energy levels in NCDs, along with the critical role of band alignment in photocatalyst design, NCDs with tunable highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) levels, is a promising candidate for hole mediator to steering hole transfer dynamics [61].

In this study, a 0D/2D/2D nanohybrid photocatalyst, NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx (Mx short for mix groups, including -O, –OH, -F), was synthesized for the first time through a facile hydrothermal route (Fig. 1 ). The structural and physiochemical properties of photocatalyst were investigated. Then, the composite was applied to the photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production and the degradation of the widely used antibiotic, tetracycline (TC). The photogenerated carrier transfer pathway on the dual co-catalyst coupled hybrid was elucidated, and the photocatalytic mechanism was clarified. In NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx , the Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs are exploited as an electron acceptor and hole mediator, respectively. The synergistic effect enhances the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanosheets by steering interfacial charge kinetics. By this means, the activities of the photooxidation and the photoreduction reactions were both enhanced effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing of Ti3C2 Mx , TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx-t, and TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: 0.75 g NaBF4 were dissolved in 15 mL 37 wt% HCl, then 1.0 g Ti3AlC2 were added and mixed uniformly. The suspension was transferred to a 100-mL autoclave and treated at 180 °C for 15 h. After that, the suspension was washed by deionized water, dried under vacuum to obtain Ti3C2 Mx (M = -O, –OH, -F) [53].

100 mg as-prepared Ti3C2 Mx and 0.165 g NaBF4 were added into 15 mL 0.1 mol/L HCl and stirred evenly, then transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, and treated at 180 °C for a certain time (6 h, 12 h, 24 h) to obtain TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx
[41,44], labeled as TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx-t.
In particular, when the hydrothermal duration was 48 h, the Ti3C2 Mx was substantially completely converted into TiO2 [41,44], so this sample was marked as TiO2, that is TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx-48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx , NCDs/Ti3C2 Mx , and NCDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx-t and a certain of NCDs solution (see Supporting Information for details by the preparation method of NCDs) were added into 20 mL absolute ethanol with magnetic stirring, then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave, and treated at 120 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the suspension was washed with deionized water, and then rinsed several times with ethanol. The separated precipitate was dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

When the amount of urea added is 0.1 g, and the loading amount of NCDs is 1.0%, different samples with different supporter of Ti3C2 Mx , TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -6 h, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -24 h were both prepared through this procedure, labeled as 0.1 NCDs/Ti3C2 Mx and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx-t, respectively. Specially, when TiO2 used as the supporter, the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 were prepared.

When the supporter was TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, and the loading amount of NCDs was 1.0%, different samples with NCDs at urea addition of 0.00, 0.02, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 g were prepared through this procedure, labeled as y NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h. Specially, when y = 0, CDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h was fabricated.

When the supporter was TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, and the NCDs at urea addition of 0.10 g, different samples with NCDs at mass percentages of 0.0%, 0.1%, 0.5%, 1.0%, 1.5%, and 2.0% were prepared through the same procedure, label as z% NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h.
Besides, the synthesis methods of other various physical mixing photocatalysts, including 0.1NCDs + TiO2 + Ti3C2 Mx and 0.1NCDs + TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, can be seen in the Supporting Information .

Photocatalytic hydrogen production experiments were conducted in a full glass automatic on-line trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd., China), and the H2 was quantitatively detected on line by a gas chromatograph (GC-9790, Fuli instruments Co., Ltd, China) via a TCD detector. The light source provided by the full spectrum of a 300 W Xenon lamp source (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight, China). 20 mg of the as-prepared photocatalyst and 0.01 M solution of H2PtCl6·6H2O (25 μL, 0.2 wt% Pt on photocatalyst) were uniformly dispersed in the aqueous solution containing 20% methanol. Five cycles were carried out to determine the stability of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h photocatalyst. For different incident monochromatic wavelengths (313, 350, 380 and 420 nm), the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) is calculated according to the equation (1):

where, n H2 is the number of molecules of H2 evolution, λ is the wavelength of the irradiation monochromatic light, Pλ and Eλ are the light intensity per unit area and the average photon energy corresponding to λ, respectively.

Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) was performed on a cylindrical photochemical reactor (Phchem III, Beijing NBeT Co., Ltd., China). 50 mg photocatalyst and 500 mL 10 mg/L tetracycline hydrochloride were put into a 1 L quartz tube. A 500 W Xenon lamp was used as light source. Before light irradiation, the mixture was stirred in the dark for 1 h to obtain the adsorption equilibrium. In the process of photocatalysis, about 5 mL samples were taken out at intervals of 30 min, and five samples after photocatalysis were obtained. The samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to remove the catalyst, and then measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 2600).

Transient photocurrent response (I-t), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky (MS) spectroscopy, and transient open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) were performed on a three-electrode system using a CHI 650E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The photocatalyst, Pt tablets and Ag/AgCl were used as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. A 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight, China) was used as the light source. 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as electrolyte solution.
The characterization and DFT calculation methods were both depicted in supporting information.

The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of 0.1 NCDs (Fig. 2
a) depicts a broad peak about 22.0°, with an interlayer spacing of 0.40 nm, which was larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm) [64] due to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups in NCDs [65]. The surface structure of 0.1 NCDs was further characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 2 b). The peaks at 1660 and 1043 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O and bending vibration of C-O-C due to the presence of carboxylic acid [65,66]. The peaks at 2922 and 2848 cm−1 are caused by the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching of CH2 and C-H, respectively [67]. The peaks located at 3428, 1570, and 1397 cm−1 are assigned to N-H (and C-OH) stretching vibrations, N-H bending vibration, and C-N bending vibration, respectively, further revealed the presence of amine groups [60,65,66]. The existence of various polar groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, etc.) on the surface of NCDs could improve the water solubility and stability of NCDs, as well as enhance the electron acceptance and transfer capability of NCDs [60].

Fig. 2 c shows the UV–Vis absorption of 0.1 NCDs and CDs. The absorption capacity of NCDs is much stronger than that of CDs, and NCDs has two characteristic peaks at 250 nm and 330 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 conjugate domains and n-π* transition of C=O band [60]. Fig. 2 d shows the up-conversion photoluminescence spectrum of 0.1 NCDs solution. The excitation is mainly from 600 nm to 900 nm, and the NCDs solution shows photoluminescence with short wavelength (350–650 nm), indicating that NCDs have excellent up-conversion photoluminescence properties [60,66], and leading to the enhanced efficiency of light utilization in the photocatalytic reaction.

As shown in the TEM image in Fig. 2 e, 0.1 NCDs with an average size of ≈ 3.54 nm are obtained (the size distribution in inset in (e) was calculated by counting 100 nanoparticles from TEM images). As shown in Fig. 2 f, a set of clear lattice fringes with an inner plane spacing of 0.32 nm, which belonged to the (002) crystal plane of graphite, indicates that NCDs have graphite carbon structure [63,68].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 3
a, b,
Figure S1
a and
Figure S2 . Two broad peaks at 9.0 and 18.0° are detected in Ti3C2 Mx , which belong to the (002) and (004) facets, respectively [51]. After the hydrothermal treatment for 12 h, the characteristic peak of Ti3C2 Mx can be observed in TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx hybrid [44]. In addition, a set of sharp diffraction peaks at 25.3, 37.0, 37.9, 38.5, 48.0, 53.9, 55.0, 62.7, and 68.8° are also detected, which are related to the (101), (103), (004), (112), (200), (105), (211), (204), and (116) reflections of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272), respectively [44]. Furthermore, Fig. 3 b shows that the (002) facet of Ti3C2 Mx has moved from 9.0 to 8.7° after the hydrothermal treatment, because the hydrothermal reaction enlarges the c lattice parameter and interlayer distance of Ti3C2 Mx . It can be seen from Figure S2 that with the extension of hydrothermal time, the diffraction peak of Ti3C2 Mx gradually decreased, replaced by diffraction signal of TiO2 increase, indicating that TiO2 in-situ transformed from Ti3C2 Mx
[41,44,69]. After a longer hydrothermal duration (48 h), only the diffraction signal of TiO2 is detected in the sample, while the broad peak of Ti3C2 Mx disappears (Fig. 3 b), which demonstrates that Ti3C2 Mx is totally oxidized into TiO2 after the long-term hydrothermal reaction. Regardless of whether TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx and TiO2 are loaded with NCDs or CDs, their XRD diffraction spectra are fully consistent with those without loading (Fig. 3 a, Figure S1
a), indicating that the loading process had no effect on the structure of the host. In addition, because the particle size and loading amount of the loaded NCDs are small, there is no signal in XRD.

The chemical structure and functional groups of the as-prepared samples were characterized by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy as displayed in Fig. 3 c. In terms of Ti3C2 Mx , the two vibrations at 2920 cm−1 and 2846 cm−1 are assigned to the C-H bond, while the signal located at 1641 cm−1 is ascribed to C=O bond on the surface. The signals at 3445 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1 are ascribed to the –OH and O-H bonds, respectively. Those results confirming that the Ti3C2 Mx surfaces are adsorbed oxygen-containing functional group, including -O and –OH groups [45]. After the hydrothermal oxidation treatment, the band appeared between 700 and 500 cm−1 is ascribed to Ti-O stretching vibration [45]. Compared the FTIR spectra of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx and TiO2, the signal of Ti-O vibration is strengthened, owing to the complete depletion of Ti3C2 Mx and oxidation to TiO2. The peak at 567 cm−1 blue shifts to 602 cm−1, revealing the interaction between Ti3C2 Mx and TiO2.

The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore distribution curve of as-prepared samples are depicted in Fig. 3 d and Fig. 3 e, respectively. Owing to the enlarge interlayer spacing of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h (Fig. 4
,
Figure S4 ) than pristine Ti3C2 Mx , the BET specific surface area (SSA) increased from 8.2 to 16.1 m2/g. While the hydrothermal duration continued to extend to 48 h (TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -48 h, namely, TiO2), the layered Ti3C2 Mx completely collapsed and fully converted into TiO2 ( Figure S3
,
Figure S4 ), therefore, its BET SSA is reduced to 4.8 m2/g. After loading NCDs, the BET specific surface area is increased slightly. The SSA of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 are 20.2 and 9.5 m2/g, respectively. The pore size distribution curve (Fig. 3 e) shows macropores in Ti3C2 Mx . TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx are dominated by mesopores, while there was no obvious pore distribution in TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 due to the oxidization-induced destruction of the 2D layered structure of Ti3C2 Mx .

Electron microscopy was applied to observe the morphologies and structures of samples. The as-prepared Ti3C2 Mx (Fig. 4 a) has an obvious two-dimensional layered structure and an accordion-like morphology [70]. The anisotropy of this structure is conducive to the transport of carriers in the photocatalytic reaction. After the hydrothermal oxidation (Fig. 4 b), square TiO2 nanosheets with (001) facets exposed in width of 300–600 nm and thickness of 50–100 nm are embedded on the layered Ti3C2 Mx to form a 2D/2D TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx heterojunction [41,44]. While after the hydrothermal treatment for 48 h, the layered Ti3C2 Mx disappears, and a stacked TiO2 nanosheets structure is obtained ( Figure S3
a), which is consistent with the XRD results (Fig. 3 a, b,
Figure S2 ) [41,44]. After loading NCDs, the morphology of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 are not significantly different from the supporter (Fig. 4 c,
Figure S3
b), indicating that the loading of NCDs does not change the morphology and structure, which is in accordancewith the results of XRD. The ratio of Ti3C2 Mx in TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -t prepared by different hydrothermal time was measured by EDS (see Figure S4
, Table S1 and S2 in the Supporting Information for details). According to the atomic ratio of each element in Ti3C2 Mx , the chemical formula can be determined as Ti3C2(OH)OF2. Furthermore, based on the balances of Ti and C atoms, the proportion of Ti3C2 Mx in TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -6 h, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -24 h and TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -48 h determined as 71%, 42%, 14% and 2%, respectively [44,45]. It further reveals that TiO2 is derived from the in-situ oxidation of Ti3C2 Mx , and Ti3C2 Mx is almost completely converted to TiO2 after hydrothermal treated for 48 h.

Fig. 4 d depicts a typical TEM image of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, clearly showed the growth of TiO2 on layered Ti3C2 Mx , while the NCDs deposited on TiO2. The unique layered texture of Ti3C2 Mx could be clearly observed in region i, while the lattice fringes of region ii and region iii were at an angle of 27.5°, which could infer that the NCDs deposited on TiO2. Magnified observation shows the fringes with the intervals of 1.157, 0.35, and 0.322 nm are clearly observed, which belong to the (002) facets of Ti3C2 Mx
[71], (101) facets of anatase TiO2 [41], and (002) facets of NCDs [63,68], respectively. The interfacial structures of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h were further depicted. Fig. 4 e shows the interface between NCDs and TiO2. It is observed that the NCDs nanodots are loaded on the TiO2 nanosheets, and the lattice fringes of 0.22, 0.32, and 0.35 nm can be clearly observed (iv-vi), corresponding to the (100), (002) facets of NCDs [60,66], and (101) facet of anatase TiO2 [41,44]. Meanwhile, Fig. 4 f depicts the interface between Ti3C2 Mx and TiO2. It is observed that TiO2 grows simultaneously on both sides of Ti3C2 Mx , and the lattice fringes of 0.24, 1.157, and 0.35 nm are clearly observed (vii-ix), corresponding to the (103) facets of anatase TiO2 [72], (002) facets of Ti3C2 Mx
[71], and (101) facet of anatase TiO2 [41,44]. The intimate contacts between NCDs/TiO2 and TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h minimize the interfacial defects and provide a guarantee for the rapid transfer of photo-generated carriers through the interface [44]. The distribution of NCDs was further verified by STEM and EDS element mapping of C, N, Ti and O. Fig. 4 g clearly indicates that the N, C, Ti, and O elements are highly uniformly distributed on the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h catalyst, which further demonstrate that NCDs are evenly dispersed on TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h.

XPS were characterized to analyze the surface chemical composition of photocatalyst. Obvious signals are detected in Ti3C2 Mx , TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h near 684, 530, 460, and 285 eV (Fig. 5
a), which are attributed to F1s, Ti 2p, O 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively. In addition, a weak signal appears near 400 eV, belonging to N 1 s, which further indicates that NCDs are successfully loaded on TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h. The peaks at 401.3, 400.3, and 399.2 eV in the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 5 f) are originated from N-H, N-C(3), and C-N = C, respectively [60,73].

Five peaks at 288.6, 286.4, 284.8, 283.2, and 282.1 eV deconvoluted at the C 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 5 b), which are ascribed to C-F, C-O, C-C, C-Ti-O, and C-Ti species, respectively [45,53], indicates that in addition to the oxygen-containing functional groups, -F is also adsorbed on the Ti3C2 Mx surface. Obviously, after Ti3C2 Mx is oxidized, the C-Ti signal shifts to the lower binding energy position. The Ti 2p signal (Fig. 5 c) can be deconvoluted into four groups of doublets (Ti 2p1/2-Ti 2p3/2), with a doublet separation of 5.7 eV and fixed area ratio 1:2 [44]. The Ti 2p3/2 components at 458.8, 457.1, 456.4 and 455.1 eV are corresponding to TiO2, TixOy, Ti-F, and Ti-C, respectively [44,53,74]. The Ti-C signal indicate the presence of Ti3C2 Mx MXene in the NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h hybrid, while the Ti-F peak reveal -F terminations adsorbed on the as-prepared Ti3C2 Mx . It can be seen in Fig. 5 d that as Ti3C2 Mx is partially oxidized into TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, the intensity of -F decreased, but there was little difference in the intensity of -F before and after loading NCDs, distinct -F can still be detected in 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h. The O1s spectra (Fig. 5 e) centered at 531.7 and 523.0 eV can be assigned as Ti-OH, and Ti-O-Ti (TiO2), respectively [44,45], which further demonstrates that –OH is also adsorbed on the surface of Ti3C2 Mx . These XPS combined with the FTIR results indicate that mix terminations adsorbed on the Ti3C2 Mx prepared by hydrothermal etching method, including -O, –OH and -F.

The as-obtained photocatalysts were subjected to photocatalytic water splitting reaction (Fig. 6
a-f). The effects of the following three factors on the HER performance were systematically investigated, including the proportion of Ti3C2 Mx in TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx (different hydrothermal duration of preparing TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx ), the amount of urea added during the preparation of NCDs, and the loading amount of NCDs. Firstly, as Fig. 6 a depicts, when the amount of urea in the preparation of NCDs is 0.1 g and the loading amount of NCDs is 1.0 wt%, the hydrogen production rate of NCDs/Ti3C2 Mx as low as 30.4 μmol g-1h−1, ascribed to that the Ti3C2 Mx MXene has metal-like property and cannot be excited [17,38]. When the hydrothermal duration of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx rose from 6 to 12 h, the hydrogen evolution rate of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx increased to 508.9 to 2056.2 μmol g-1h−1 (Fig. 6 a), while the proportion of Ti3C2 Mx in TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx is 71% and 42%, respectively (Table S2). While further reduce the content of Ti3C2 Mx from 42% to 2%, namely, prolonged the hydrothermal time from 12 to 48 h (Table S2), its hydrogen production diminished from 2056.2 to 1261.3 μmol g-1h−1. It indicating that the optimum content of Ti3C2 Mx in the TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx is 42% (hydrothermal duration is 12 h), it could be attributed to that this supporter (TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h) has the most TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx heterojunction [44,69]. Secondly, in order to explore the effect of N content of NCDs on the photocatalytic performance of the composites, b NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx (b is urea addition) composites with different urea addition is synthesized and applies to HER reaction. As depicts in Fig. 6 b, over the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx photocatalyst, the hydrogen production is maximized at an average rate of 2056.2 μmol g-1h−1. However, as the amount of urea is further increased (0.25–0.5 g), the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate activity decreases (1378.1–1022.1 μmol g-1h−1). It should be ascribe that excessive N atom concentrations could decrease effective surface contact areas between carbon and TiO2, which limits carrier transfer [60]. Thirdly, the HER reaction of different amounts NCDs coupled with TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx (z% NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx , z% is loading amount) are also conducted (Fig. 6 c), the HER gradually enhances with the increase of NCDs concentration. The highest HER rate (2056.2 μmol g-1h−1) is obtained for 1% NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx , however, further NCDs loading increases (>1.0 wt%) resulted in decrease of HER rates, which might be attributed to the NCDs inner filter effect via competition for the absorption of photons with TiO2, thereby reducing the excitation of carriers [60].

Furthermore, the photocatalyst design can be rationalized by a series of control experiments shown in Fig. 6 d and 6e. TiO2 (namely, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -48 h) has a limited hydrogen production activity (2610 μmol g−1 for 5 h, 521.9 μmol g-1h−1), and activity of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h increased to 4726 μmol g−1 for 5 h (945.2 μmol g-1h−1), however, Ti3C2 Mx itself has hardly no catalytic activity due to its metal-like properties [75], which clearly clarifies that Ti3C2 Mx acts as a cocatalyst in the photocatalytic water splitting reaction [41,42]. As shown in Fig. 6 e, the hydrogen production activity of dual carbon material hybrid 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h (2056.2 μmol g-1h−1), is higher than that of single carbon material composited photocatalysts (TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 are 945.2 and 1261.4 μmol g-1h−1, respectively), and much higher than that of pure TiO2 (521.9 μmol g-1h−1), indicating that NCDs and Ti3C2 Mx could synergistically enhance the photocatalytic reduction reaction activity of TiO2. It is inspiring that the optimized photocatalyst 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h (2056.2 μmol g-1h−1) has 4.01 and 1.65 folds the catalytic activity of TiO2 (512.9 μmol g-1h−1) and commercial P25 (1243.2 μmol g-1h−1), respectively (Fig. 6 e). In addition, the HER rates of physical mixture photocatalysts, 0.1 NCDs + TiO2 + Ti3C2 Mx and 0.1 NCDs + TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are 611.7 μmol g-1h−1 and 1177.1 μmol g-1h−1, which are both markedly lower than 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, which reveals that in-situ growth of TiO2 on Ti3C2 Mx and hydrothermal loading NCDs on TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h can minimize the interfacial defects, subsequently promotes the HER activity [41,69]. Furthermore, the HER performance of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h is not only higher than the TiO2/C-based hybrid photocatalysts, but also superior than the recently reported MXene-based photocatalysts, a more detailed comparison is shown in Figure S1 . As depicted in Fig. 6 f, AQY values 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h of under different monochromatic light wavelengths of 313, 350, 380 and 420 nm are calculated to be 3.86%, 9.21%, 0.60%, and 0, respectively, indicate exhibits significant wavelength dependence via light-harvesting property [76]. Finally, the stability and reusability experiments of the optimal 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h photocatalyst are carried out. After five consecutive experiments, the HER performance almost no fluctuation occurs (Fig. 6 g), which indicates excellent stability of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h composites.

Meanwhile, the as-obtained photocatalysts were also used in the photocatalytic oxidation reaction, degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), which is a new type of organic pollutant [77]. As shown in Figure S5 and Fig. 6 h, after 60 min of dark adsorption equilibrium, the photocatalysts start the photocatalytic degradation reaction of TC. The apparent reaction rate constant (k) of the degradation reaction was obtained according to the equation (2) [77]:
(2)
- l n ( C /
C 0
) = k t
where C0 and C are the initial concentration and real-time concentration of TC, respectively. t is the irradiation time (min), and k denotes the apparent reaction rate constant (min−1).

After 150 min of photocatalytic reaction perform on TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, the C/C0 of TC is 0.85 (k is 1.07 × 10-3 min−1). When loading NCDs (or CDs), the photocatalyst accelerated the photodegradation reaction rate of TC ( Figure S4 ). Among them, the optimal catalyst is 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, the C/C0 of TC decrease to 0.55 after irradiation for 150 min, and its k value reaches 3.64 × 10-3 min−1.

As shown in Fig. 6 i, the k of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are 1.07 × 10-3 and 3.64 × 10-3 min−1, respectively, which are much higher than that without MXene (the k for TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 are 0.40 × 10-3 and 2.68 × 10-3, respectively), indicating that Ti3C2 Mx MXene, as the charge mediator, is a decisive factor to enhance photocatalytic degradation activity. The degradation reaction rate of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h is 9.1 folds of TiO2, which also demonstrates the effectiveness of the dual co-catalyst strategy on photocatalytic oxidation reaction activity of TiO2.

The photoluminescence and photoelectrochemical performance of as-obtained photocatalysts were determined to understand the roles of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs. Photoluminescence (PL) emission results from the recombination of free carriers could be used to investigate the electron-hole fate in photocatalysts. The PL emission spectra (Fig. 7
a) of as-prepared samples were investigated at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The resulted PL peak at about 639 nm is ascribed to the emission of band gap transition of anatase TiO2 [78]. The intensity of PL spectra from TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 are lower than that of TiO2, which is ascribed to the photogenerated carrier mediator of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs [41,42]. Furthermore, the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h photocatalyst exhibits the lowest fluorescence intensity, which indicates that the photocatalyst has the optimum electron-hole separation efficiency. Furthermore, from the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra depicted in Fig. 7 b, it is observed the average decay lifetime of the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h composite is 0.1247 ns, which is prolonged compared with that of TiO2 (0.0911 ns), 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 (0.0943 ns), and TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h (0.0924 ns), suggesting that an effective electron-hole pair transport and charge separation process are achieved [42].

Fig. 7 d, e shows the photocurrent of photocatalysts under Xe lamp irradiation with light on/off cycles. The photocurrent responses of pure TiO2 are quite low, which may stem from the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs. When a carbon-based cocatalyst is coupled to TiO2, its photocurrent increases to varying degrees. The order of specific photocurrent from high to low is 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, 0.1 NCDs/TiO2, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, and TiO2. Especially, the 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h composite (5 μA cm−2) considerably enhances photocurrent density for 100 times than TiO2 (0.05 μA cm−2), undoubtedly revealing that the synergy of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs accelerates the transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [44]. The charge transport resistances of photocatalysts were evaluated by EIS. As shown in Fig. 7 c, TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1NCDs/TiO2 have smaller radius in their Nyquist plots than TiO2, indicating that the transport efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs has been enhanced in the presence of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs [42]. Among those photocatalysts, 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h shows the smallest radius of Nyquist curve, which suggests the minimum charge transfer resistance due to the electron-hole separation channels on the photocatalyst [42].

To further confirm the above results, open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) was also performed. The OCVD technology is applicable for the charge dynamics at the photocatalyst-electrode–electrolyte (μs to s) junctions [42,44]. The open-circuit voltage (VOC) corresponds to the increase of quasi-Fermi level of the semiconductor after photoexcitation (E*F,n) with respect to the Fermi level in the dark condition (E F,0), as described by equation (3):

where, q represents the charge of elementary charge, k B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin, and n and n0 are the photoinduced charge density in the semiconductor photocatalyst under non-equilibrium state and equilibrium state, respectively [79,80].

As shown in Fig. 7 f, the changes in photovoltage of photocatalyst containing MXene (0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h) much slower rate after light on than the parallel catalyst without MXene (0.1 NCDs/TiO2 and TiO2). This could be attributed to the Ti3C2 Mx that acts as a cocatalyst, where a large number of photogenerated carrier is transferred on the surface of the photocatalyst ​and neutralized with the electron flow of the charge circuit.

Furthermore, the charge dynamics lifetime (τ n) can be quantitatively calculated through the V OC decay after light off by the equation (4):

where τ n represents the average electron lifetime and Voc is the open-circuit voltage at time [42,44,79,80].

As shown in Fig. 7 f1, the electron dynamics lifetime of TiO2 is in the range from 10 to 104 s, which appeared at the moment lighting off. It was widely accepted that the relatively long-lived distribution of τ n was an important signal of the effective separation of photoinduced electrons and holes. The electron dynamics lifetimes of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2 have been extended by the carrier mediator effect. The charge dynamics lifetime of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h was the longest, reaching 102 to 104 s, which was an order of magnitude higher than that of TiO2. In the low voltage region, τn -V oc curve is relatively smooth (Fig. 7 f1) because E* F,n rapidly decays and returns to equilibrium state (E F,0 ) after the lamp is turned off (Fig. 7 f). On the contrary, in the high voltage region, E* F,n recovered to E F,0 after light off for a long time, and V oc attenuated slowly (Fig. 7 f), so the τn -V oc curve fluctuated obviously (Fig. 7 f1).The above results unambiguously demonstrated the effective spatial charge separation, the slow recombination of electron/hole pairs and the long photogenerated carrier lifetime over 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, which promoted by the unique 0D/2D/2D structure and the dual-cocatalyst strategy [41,42].

In order to further reveal the mechanism of the improved photocatalytic performance of 0.1NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, density-functional theory (DFT) calculation and ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) were carried out. As Fig. 8
a, b show, Ti3C2 MXene exhibits metallic characteristics with continuous electronic states crossing Fermi level, indicate its excellent electrical conductivity, and the work function (Φ) of naked Ti3C2 is 3.55 eV. In fact, the actual work function of MXene is significantly restricted by its surface adsorption groups, and different preparation routes will have different surface chemical compositions. Therefore, the effect of surface adsorbed groups on the work function of MXene was investigated in detail by DFT calculations. The results show that the work function of Ti3C2F2 slightly increases to 4.704 eV (Fig. 8 c), when the surface groups are –OH and -O, the work functions of Ti3C2O2 and Ti3C2(OH)2 change significantly, to 1.877 (Fig. 8 d) and 5.936 eV (Fig. 8 e), respectively [36,37]. The work function of MXenes simultaneous adsorption of multiple groups were further explored. The work functions of Ti3C2(OH)F and Ti3C2OF are 2.355 (Fig. 8 f) and 5.814 eV (Fig. 8 g), respectively, when the surface adsorption group are -F, –OH and -O coexist, its work function is 3.626 eV (Fig. 8 h), slightly higher than that of naked Ti3C2.

Furthermore, the UPS result shows that the actual work function of Ti3C2 Mx MXene is 4.16 eV (Fig. 8 i), larger than naked Ti3C2, which is consistent with the DFT calculation result. In addition, the work functions of TiO2 and NCDs are 3.98 and 3.09 eV, respectively. It is well known that the work function is the energy difference between the Fermi level (E F) and the vacuum level (E vac). Therefore, the Fermi levels of Ti3C2 Mx , TiO2, and NCDs are −4.16, −3.98, and −3.09 eV, respectively, indicating that the electron trapping and hole mediator of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs, respectively.

The matched band structure is a key factor to ensure the photocatalytic reactions, therefore, the band structure of the photocatalyst was analyzed by means of DRUV–vis spectra and Mott-Schottky (M−S) spectra. The optical absorption was characterized by UV–Vis DRS (Fig. 9
a). TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h exhibited abroad absorption from ultraviolet region to visible region. In contrast to TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h shows an obvious red-shift and strong absorption in the visible light band. The absorption capacity of TiO2 in the ultraviolet region has been significantly enhanced because the increase of TiO2 content at long-term hydrothermal duration. The band gaps of as-prepared TiO2 were further elucidated by Tauc’s plot. As Fig. 9 b depicts, the band gap estimated from the intercept is 2.98 eV for TiO2.

The M−S curve of TiO2 in Fig. 9 c exhibits positive slope, because TiO2 is an n-type semiconductor [81,82], and the abscissa intercept of TiO2 is −0.837 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The carrier densities of photocatslysts could be estimated by equation (5):

Where Nd, e0, ε, ε0 and d(1/C2)/dV represent the carrier density, electron charge, the dielectric coefficient (55 for anatase), the permittivity of vacuum, and the straight slope obtained from the plot, respectively [81,82].

The average carrier densities of TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are calculated to be 4.51 × 1020 and 4.76 × 1020 cm−1. It further demonstrates that the electron trapping of Ti3C2 Mx and hole mediator of NCDs effectively synergistic suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The flat band potential for TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are −0.837 and −0.908 V versus Ag/AgCl at pH = 6.5, respectively, which are obtained from the Mott-Schottky plots. Meanwhile, the acquired flat-band potential could be regarded as their conduction band (CB) potential (E CB) [25,42]. Namely, the E CB for TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are −0.257 and −0.328 V vs the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) at pH = 0, respectively, convert by equations (6) and (7):

Integrated with the band gap of TiO2 via DRS result (Fig. 9 b), the valence band (VB) potentials (E VB) of TiO2 and 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h are calculated to be 2.723 and 2.652 V vs. NHE, respectively. These data coupled with their work function (Ef ) are helpful to interpret the photocatalysis mechanism of the photocatalyst, which will be discussed later.

In order to further reveal the mechanism of the improved photocatalytic performance for 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h, the electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was conducted to detect its ability to produce active oxygen species (ROS) (Fig. 9 e, f). DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) as a spin trapping molecule was used to detect the hydroxyl radical (•OH) in aqueous solution and superoxide radical (•O2 –) in methanol solution [83]. As observed, the signals of peaks for both DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 – are not detected when the system is not irradiated, suggesting that no •OH and •O2 – are generated by 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h in the darkness. In contrast, it is clearly found that the characteristic signals of DMPO-•OH (Fig. 9 e) and DMPO-•O2 – (Fig. 9 f) appear and become stronger upon simulated solar light irradiation [77], revealing the NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h hybrid can quickly produce a large amount of •OH and •O2 – under irradiation.

Based on above experimental results, the photocatalytic mechanism over the NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx could be rationalized in the schematic diagram (Fig. 10 ). Before contacting between TiO2, Ti3C2 Mx , and NCDs, the E F potential of TiO2 (-3.98 eV) is more negative than that of NCDs (-3.09 eV), and more positive than that of Ti3C2 Mx (-4.16 eV). The photoexcited electrons of TiO2 migrate to the conduction band (-0.257 V vs. NHE), leaving photogenerated holes in the valence band (2.723 V vs. NHE). However, in this case, the electron-hole pairs are prone to bulk recombination, resulting in low photocatalytic efficiency.

While TiO2, Ti3C2 Mx , and NCDs are hybridized together, E F of TiO2, Ti3C2 Mx , and NCDs would be negative shift and E F of Ti3C2 Mx would be shift to the same Fermi level position in order to achieve equilibrium [41,42]. In this situation, under light irradiation, electron-hole pairs are formed between the conduction band (-0.328 V vs. NHE) and valence band (2.652 V vs. NHE) of TiO2 with the photo-excitation. As Ti3C2 Mx MXene possess metallic properties and positive Fermi level (E F) than TiO2. Therefore, “Schottky barriers” was formed at the interface of TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx , allowing only the movement of electrons from TiO2 to Ti3C2 Mx and preventing backflow of electrons to recombination [29,39,84] As for the interface of NCDs/TiO2, owing to the work function of NCDs is 3.09 eV, much larger than the work function for TiO2 (3.98 eV), clear suggests a transfer of electrons from NCDs to TiO2, while the direction of hole transfer is opposite to that of electrons. Under irradiation, the electrons will be excited to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 and the LUMO of NCDs, leaving photogenerated holes on the VB/HOMO, respectively. Thereafter, the electrons from the LUMO band of NCDs quickly transfer to the CB of the TiO2 due to its deeper energy level, and the holes left in the VB of TiO2 then transfer to the NCDs, where it can participate in oxidation reactions [62,63,85]. The simultaneous separation of electrons and holes greatly suppresses the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and elongated the lifetime of carriers [41,42,86]. The electron trapping effect of metallic Ti3C2 Mx , and hole mediator role of NCDs effective steering interfacial charge kinetics, synergistically enhance the photocatalytic activity of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx hybrids.

Nanosized NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx hybrid photocatalyst with unique 0D/2D/2D structure is successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal route. The optimum photocatalyst (0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h) has a high photocatalytic HER activity (2056.2 μmol g-1h−1), which is 4.01 times higher than that of TiO2 (512.9 μmol g-1h−1) and 1.65 folds of that of Degussa P25 TiO2 (1243.2 μmol g-1h−1). Meanwhile, photocatalytic degradation reaction rate toward tetracycline hydrochloride of 0.1 NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx -12 h is 9.1 folds of TiO2. Surface chemistry and UPS spectra indicate that the as-prepared Ti3C2 Mx exhibits mix surface termination (-O, –OH, -F), and the work function is 4.16 eV. The photocatalytic reaction of NCDs/TiO2/Ti3C2 Mx follows an opposite-carrier-separation mechanism, where NCDs act as a hole mediator and Ti3C2 Mx as an electron acceptor to enhance the separation of photogenerated charges. Meanwhile, the highly active (001) TiO2 facets, and the excellent conductivity of Ti3C2 Mx and NCDs significantly improve the photocatalytic performance. This work provides new insights into the design and preparation of the highly efficient MXene-based photocatalyst.
